perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Delaware may impact the enforceability of certain provisions depending on the specific laws involved. For instance, California has specific regulations concerning non-compete agreements that are stricter than Delaware law. While not a direct contradiction, it can affect the enforcement of contractual obligations under different legal standards. This is because California law prohibits most non-compete agreements, whereas Delaware law may enforce them under certain circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that "Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." By selecting Delaware law, the enforceability of non-compete agreements could be interpreted differently, potentially conflicting with California's strong public policy against restraints of trade if the agreement were to be enforced in California or against a California resident.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from California to Delaware. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly voids contracts that restrain someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business, subject to certain exceptions. The Law Explanation indicates that Delaware law may enforce non-compete agreements under circumstances where California would not, potentially conflicting with California's policy against restraints of trade if the agreement were enforced in California or against a California resident. Therefore, selecting Delaware law creates a potential contradiction with California law, especially concerning non-compete clauses, as it could lead to the enforcement of agreements that would be void under BPC 16600. The change directly undermines California's public policy as expressed in the statute.

perturbation.original_text: You understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by your or your representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity provided however any suit for damages shall be specifically limited to actual provable damages, and Company hereby waives any and all rights to seek consequential, punitive, treble or other compensatory damages of any types or kind.
perturbation.changed_text: You understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives, and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall be deemed the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives and shall exclude all other remedies available at law or equity, and Company hereby waives any and all rights to seek any type of damages.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity' and replacing it with 'shall be deemed the exclusive remedies' and 'and shall exclude all other remedies' significantly limits the remedies available to the Company, potentially violating basic principles of contract law depending on the severity of the breach and the circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1668 states that "All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt anyone from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law." By making specific performance and injunctive relief the *exclusive* remedies and waiving all other types of damages, the modified text could be interpreted as an attempt to exempt the breaching party from responsibility for their actions, especially if those actions constitute fraud, willful injury, or violation of law. The exclusion of any and all rights to seek *any* type of damages, even in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct, could violate California's public policy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts California Civil Code Section 1668, as it attempts to limit the remedies available to the Company, potentially exempting the breaching party from responsibility for their actions, even in cases of fraud, willful injury, or violation of law. The original text allowed for remedies at law or equity in addition to specific performance and injunctive relief, while the changed text makes these remedies exclusive and waives all other types of damages. According to the scraped law snippet, "All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law." By waiving 'any and all rights to seek any type of damages,' the modified text could be construed as an attempt to circumvent responsibility for such actions, thus violating the cited California Civil Code section.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Company furnishing you with the Evaluation Materials (as defined below) you agree as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Company furnishing you with the Evaluation Materials (as defined below) you agree as follows, provided that any dispute arising from this agreement must be resolved through binding arbitration in Delaware and Eastgroup Properties Inc. agrees to pay all arbitration costs.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing one party (Eastgroup Properties Inc.) to pay *all* arbitration costs regardless of the outcome of the arbitration could be deemed unconscionable and violate standard principles of fairness in arbitration agreements, particularly if the agreement is governed by California law where the costs are usually split between the parties..
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1284.2
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1284.2 states, "Unless the arbitration agreement otherwise provides or unless the parties to the arbitration otherwise agree by an agreement in writing executed by them at any time: (a) The fees and expenses of neutral arbitrators shall be paid as provided in Section 1141.21. (b) All other expenses of arbitration, including (but not limited to) the expense of any witness or the cost of producing documentary evidence, shall be paid as provided in Section 1141.215." Section 1141.21 essentially divides responsibility of the fees between the parties. Forcing Eastgroup to pay all costs contradicts the standard principle of cost-sharing absent a specific agreement made at the time of arbitration, and could be deemed an unfair or unconscionable term, making it unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1284.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 3. Conduct of Arbitration Proceedings [1282 - 1284.3] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1284.2. Unless the arbitration agreement otherwise provides or the parties to the arbitration otherwise agree, each party to the arbitration shall pay his pro rata share of the expenses and fees of the neutral arbitrator, together with other expenses of the arbitration incurred or approved by the neutral arbitrator, not including counsel fees or witness fees or other expenses incurred by a party for his own benefit. (Added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clause forcing Eastgroup Properties Inc. to pay all arbitration costs, which contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1284.2. The scraped law snippet states that unless the arbitration agreement provides otherwise, each party shall pay their pro rata share of the expenses and fees of the neutral arbitrator. The modified contract explicitly shifts the entire cost burden to one party, thereby contradicting the default cost-sharing arrangement prescribed by California law.
